guardrail_evaluations.explanation: The proposed 30% discount exceeds the mid-market soft ceiling of 20%. This is a yellow flag that should prompt the team to pressure-test whether the full concession is necessary to retain the logo.

The proposed discount of exactly 30% sits at the hard boundary of the 20–30% Finance approval band. This deal technically passes the threshold (<=30) but Finance sign-off is mandatory before proceeding — no further discounting is permitted without re-escalation.
No ramp schedule is present on this deal, so ramp length is treated as 0 months — well within the 3-month mid-market ceiling. This guardrail passes cleanly.

At $280K proposed price, estimated gross margin is approximately 14.3% — far below the 40% warn threshold. The deal is severely margin-compressed and warrants a full concession reshape before signing.

Estimated gross margin of ~14.3% falls well below the absolute floor of 25%. This is a hard block — the deal cannot proceed as structured without Finance and executive approval to reshape or override. Accepting all three customer requests simultaneously (commit reduction + usage-based overage + net-60) produces a structurally non-viable margin profile.

alternative_structures.label: Smaller Discount + 24-Month Commitment Lock
alternative_structures.description: Reduce the commit by 20% (to $320K) instead of 30%, but lock the customer into a 24-month renewal term with a 5% escalator in Year 2. Payment terms stay at net-30 but a one-time net-45 grace period is offered for this invoice only. Retain the usage-based overage model as requested.
alternative_structures.rationale: Trading a longer term for a smaller discount recovers $40K ACV and lifts gross margin to exactly the 25% absolute floor — passable but still requiring Finance eyes. The 24-month lock significantly reduces churn risk from the YC competitor, and the escalator in Year 2 partially offsets today's pricing concession. One-time payment grace is a low-cost goodwill gesture that avoids permanently extending terms.

alternative_structures.label: Usage-Commit Reset with Tiered Overage Upside
alternative_structures.description: Reset the committed baseline to $300K (reflecting actual ~60% utilization against the old $400K commit, i.e., ~$240K in real consumption, with a modest uplift). Structure as a pure usage-based hybrid: $300K floor commit with tiered overage at $0.0008/unit up to 120% of commit, then $0.0006/unit above that to incentivize growth. Net-60 granted. No additional flat discount beyond the commit reset.
alternative_structures.rationale: Reframes the conversation from 'discount' to 'right-sizing' — which is more defensible internally and less precedent-setting. The lower overage rate above 120% of commit creates a genuine incentive for Northbeam to grow usage rather than churn, offering upside ACV if adoption improves. Net-60 is granted as the sole non-price concession. Still below the 25% margin floor so Finance approval is required, but the growth optionality is a structural win.

alternative_structures.label: 20% Discount + Adoption Success Plan (PS Attach)
alternative_structures.description: Grant a 20% discount on the base commit ($320K), hold net-30 payment terms, and attach a $40K professional services engagement (billed separately at full margin) focused on driving adoption of the 3 unadopted features. Total contract value is $360K. The PS scope includes a dedicated CSM-led onboarding sprint and a 90-day adoption milestone. In exchange, Northbeam agrees to a co-marketing case study upon hitting the milestone.
alternative_structures.rationale: This is the only structure that addresses the root cause — low adoption — rather than just reducing price. The PS attach brings total contract value to $360K (vs. $280K in the CSM proposal), recovering $80K ACV versus the current ask. PS is billed at full margin, improving blended deal economics. The case study creates reference value that partially offsets the logo retention cost. Requires the AE to reframe the conversation with Northbeam's CRO around a success plan rather than a price war.

pricing.reasoning_summary: This deal is a hard block on the gross margin absolute floor guardrail: at $280K proposed price against a $400K list, effective gross margin is estimated at ~14.3% — well below the 25% absolute floor and the 40% warn threshold. All margin estimates use the working assumption that list price carries a 40% gross margin (COGS = $240K); the CFO should validate this figure before relying on the margin outputs as gospel. The 30% discount also sits precisely at the Finance sign-off boundary, meaning even if the margin issue were waived, this deal requires formal Finance approval before execution. The three alternative structures each address a different axis: term extension, usage-based right-sizing, and PS attach to resolve the underlying adoption problem driving the at-risk renewal — the last being the most strategically sound given that Northbeam's health score of 42 is driven by underutilization, not pricing.

performance_obligations.name: Platform subscription (committed base)
performance_obligations.description: 12-month subscription to the platform at a reduced commit of $280K, covering access and standard included usage credits.
performance_obligations.rationale: Stand-ready obligation to provide platform access over the 12-month term; customer benefits evenly across the period (ASC 606-10-25-19).

performance_obligations.name: Usage-based overage
performance_obligations.description: Consumption above the committed base billed at $0.0008/unit under the new hybrid model.
performance_obligations.rationale: Variable consideration tied to actual usage; recognized as usage occurs under the series guidance / right-to-invoice expedient (ASC 606-10-55-18).

variable_consideration_flags.explanation: Given customer only consumed 60% of prior commit, overages are unlikely in the near term but possible. Variability resolved each period as usage is metered.
The 30% commit reduction is a renewal-level repricing rather than mid-term modification, but signals downward pressure on transaction price going forward.
Net-60 terms on a 12-month contract are unlikely to constitute a significant financing component under ASC 606-10-32-18, but should be confirmed.

contract_modification_risk.explanation: This is a renewal (a new contract for a new term), not a mid-term modification. No MFN, no TFC, no expansion-pricing lock disclosed. Standard renewal accounting applies.

recognized_revenue_schedule.notes: Ratable recognition of $280K committed base over 12 months (~$23,333/month).
Recognized as incurred at $0.0008/unit; expected to be minimal given 60% historical utilization.

red_flags.label: Steep 30% discount on at-risk renewal
red_flags.explanation: Large concession driven by under-utilization and competitive pressure may set SSP precedent affecting future allocations and renewals.

red_flags.label: Shift to hybrid (usage-based overage)
red_flags.explanation: Move from pure commit to hybrid changes revenue pattern mix; ensure metering, billing, and rev-rec systems support usage-based recognition.

red_flags.label: Net-60 payment terms
red_flags.explanation: Confirm no significant financing component and assess collectibility (ASC 606-10-25-1(e)) given customer health score of 42/100.

red_flags.label: Collectibility / customer health
red_flags.explanation: Health score 42 and unadopted features raise collectibility and renewal-probability concerns; may impact contract existence criterion.

asc606.reasoning_summary: The renewal contains two distinct POs — a ratable platform subscription ($280K) and a usage-based overage component at $0.0008/unit — with revenue recognized ratably and as-consumed respectively. Key judgments involve the discount magnitude (SSP impact), the hybrid pricing shift, net-60 terms, and collectibility given a 42 health score, but no mid-term modification risk exists since this is a fresh 12-month renewal. This is an automated estimate; final determination requires CFO/auditor review.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Customer requests a 30% reduction in committed spend from $400K to $280K ACV for the renewal term, citing under-utilization (60% of commit consumed in prior term).
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: Accepting a 30% commit reduction at face value rewards under-utilization and sets a precedent that Clay will always true-down to actual consumption, eliminating any floor discipline on commits. The mechanism of harm: if Clay grants $120K off a $400K book on utilization grounds, every under-utilizing mid-market account now has a negotiating playbook. At 42/100 health score the account is also at elevated churn risk even after the discount, meaning Clay takes the revenue hit without guaranteed retention.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Clay agrees to reduce the Annual Commit to $320,000 (20% reduction, not 30%) for the FY26 renewal term, conditioned on Customer completing a mandatory Success Accelerator onboarding session for the three (3) currently unadopted paid features within sixty (60) days of contract execution. If Customer's platform utilization does not reach 80% of the $320,000 commit by the end of Q3 FY26 as measured by Clay's usage dashboard, Clay reserves the right to require a remediation plan prior to any subsequent renewal discussion. No further commit reductions shall be granted in the subsequent renewal term absent written agreement signed by Clay's VP of Revenue.
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Accept the $280K commit floor (30% reduction) only if Customer agrees to a 24-month term (not 12) at $280K Year 1 with a contractual step-up to $340K in Year 2, and completion of the Success Accelerator program is a condition of the Year 2 pricing lock. This converts a one-year discount into a multi-year retention instrument.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay has granted utilization-based commit reductions in renewals but typically caps true-downs at 15–20%; a 30% reduction in a single renewal cycle is at the high end of observed precedent.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Customer requests that any consumption above the new reduced commit be billed at a usage-based overage rate ($0.0008/unit) rather than requiring a commit uplift, eliminating any obligation to pre-commit to incremental volume.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: Shifting overage to pure usage-based pricing removes Clay's ability to capture expansion revenue through commit uplifts. If Northbeam's utilization recovers (plausible given the new Incrementality product launch driving advertiser demand for attribution), Clay captures that upside only at $0.0008/unit with no floor — and the customer faces no incentive to buy up to a larger commit. This also creates a pricing inconsistency if Clay is trying to move the market toward hybrid commit models.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Overage above the Annual Commit of $[X] shall be billed at $0.0008 per unit; provided, however, that (a) overage consumption exceeding 120% of the Annual Commit in any rolling 90-day period shall trigger a mandatory commit renegotiation at rates no less favorable than the current per-unit overage rate, and (b) Customer may not carry overage-only pricing into a subsequent renewal term — any renewal must include a commit of no less than the prior term's actual consumption.
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Accept pure usage-based overage at $0.0008/unit with no renegotiation trigger, but require that the subsequent renewal commit be set at no less than 90% of the sum of (prior term commit + overage units consumed), preventing a permanent reset to a lower base.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay has offered usage-based overage in hybrid deals but typically pairs it with a ratchet or renegotiation trigger above 120–125% of commit; the no-renegotiation version has been resisted in prior cycles.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Customer requests extension of invoice payment terms from Net-30 to Net-60, adding approximately 30 days of cash flow impact per invoice cycle.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: Net-60 on a $280K ACV deal creates a ~$23K monthly cash float impact — manageable but non-trivial given Clay's own working capital needs. The larger risk is precedent: granting Net-60 to a distressed (health score 42) account increases Days Sales Outstanding and elevates bad-debt exposure if Northbeam churns or restructures before payment.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Payment terms shall be Net-45 (not Net-60), with annual invoicing due within forty-five (45) days of the invoice date. Late payments shall accrue interest at 1.5% per month. Clay reserves the right to revert to Net-30 terms in any subsequent renewal term absent a written amendment.
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Accept Net-60 only if Customer agrees to annual upfront invoicing (full $280K due within 60 days of contract execution), eliminating mid-year cash risk by collapsing the float into a single payment event.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay has granted Net-45 to several mid-market accounts; Net-60 has been accepted only for enterprise deals above $500K ACV with strong health scores.

redline.one_line_summary: We can retain Northbeam, but we should counter at $320K (not $280K) with a Success Accelerator condition, cap Net-60 at Net-45 or flip to annual upfront, and require a renewal commit ratchet on the usage-based overage to protect future expansion.
redline.reasoning_summary: All three customer requests are recognizable patterns with established Clay precedent and clear counter-positions. The headline risk is the 30% commit reduction: at 42/100 health, Clay absorbs a $120K ACV haircut without a structural mechanism to recover it, and grants a utilization-true-down playbook to the broader book. The usage-based overage is a medium risk primarily because Northbeam's new Incrementality product launch signals potential consumption recovery — Clay should preserve upside capture through a renegotiation trigger. Payment terms are the lowest priority but should be negotiated to Net-45 or annual upfront given the account's distressed health score.

required_approvers.rationale: Default rule applies to all deals; AE Manager is the entry point for any approval chain.
Effective discount of 30% exceeds the 25% threshold, triggering mandatory CFO review.

required_approvers.rule_triggered: Non-standard payment terms route to Finance
required_approvers.rationale: Non-standard clause 'payment_terms_net_60' is present, requiring Finance sign-off on cash flow and DSO impact.
Deal ACV of $280K falls within the $100K–$500K band, requiring RevOps consistency check.

required_approvers.rule_triggered: Gross margin absolute floor violation + Pricing guardrail override requirement
required_approvers.rationale: Pricing Agent flagged a 'block_absolute' guardrail violation: estimated gross margin of 14.3% falls below the 25% absolute floor. Executive override is required to proceed with a structurally non-viable deal.

blockers_to_address_first: Pricing Agent's gross margin absolute floor guardrail is violated (14.3% vs. 25% minimum). Deal structure must be reshaped before CEO can even consider override — Pricing Agent's three alternative structures (smaller discount + 24-month lock, usage-commit reset with tiered overage, or 20% discount + PS attach) all restore margin to >= 25% and should be pressure-tested with the customer before resubmitting.

Redline Agent flagged commit_reduction at 'high' risk: 30% true-down to actual consumption sets a precedent that rewards under-utilization and eliminates commit floor discipline. Counter at $320K (20% reduction) conditioned on mandatory Success Accelerator onboarding for the three unadopted features within 60 days, or accept the $280K floor only if Northbeam commits to 24 months at $280K Year 1 + $340K Year 2 with adoption as a condition. Current $280K / 12-month proposal is legally and commercially indefensible.

Redline Agent flagged usage_based_overage at 'medium' risk: pure usage-based pricing with no renegotiation trigger at 120%+ of commit removes expansion capture and creates pricing inconsistency. Insert a mandatory commit renegotiation trigger if overage exceeds 120% of Annual Commit in any rolling 90-day period, or require that subsequent renewal commit be no less than 90% of prior term (commit + overage units consumed).

Redline Agent flagged payment_terms at 'low' risk but elevated by health score of 42: Net-60 on a distressed account increases DSO and bad-debt exposure. Counter with Net-45 or require annual upfront invoicing ($280K due within 60 days of execution) to eliminate mid-year cash float risk.

ASC 606 red flag: collectibility concern due to health score of 42 and feature unadoption. CFO must confirm that the contract meets the 'probable collection' threshold under ASC 606-10-25-1(e); if high churn risk is confirmed, revenue recognition may need to be deferred or reduced.
approval.one_line_summary: This deal routes AE Manager → RevOps + Finance + CFO (parallel) → CEO and expects ~6 business days, but is currently blocked: gross margin of 14.3% violates the 25% absolute floor and the deal must be restructured (e.g., 20% discount + 24-month lock, or smaller discount + PS attach) before CEO can consider override; commit reduction should be countered at 20% with Success Accelerator conditions; usage-based overage needs a renegotiation ratchet; and net-60 payment terms should be negotiated to net-45 or annual upfront given the account's 42/100 health score.
approval.reasoning_summary: Five rules fired, creating a standard escalation chain: AE Manager entry → RevOps + Finance + CFO in parallel (triggered by ACV, non-standard payment terms, and discount >25%) → CEO (triggered by margin floor violation requiring executive override). The Pricing Agent's 'block_absolute' guardrail on gross margin is the decisive blocker: at 14.3% margin, the deal is structurally non-viable and must be reshaped before it can proceed. The Redline Agent's 'high' priority on commit reduction and 'medium' priority on usage-based overage signal legal and commercial defenses that the AE must negotiate before resubmitting. ASC 606 flagged collectibility risk tied to the 42 health score, which CFO must validate. The deal is approvable in principle (all rules map to standard roles) but cannot proceed as currently structured.

text.text: FY26 Renewal — Commit Reduction Request · Northbeam Mortgage

fields.text:
*Approval Chain*
AE Mgr → RevOps + Finance + CFO (parallel) → CEO · ~6 days

text.text:
*What the deal team needs to know*

• *Hard block on margin floor — deal cannot proceed as structured.* Estimated gross margin of 14.3% violates the 25% absolute floor, requiring CEO override. The deal must be restructured before submission: best paths are (a) 20% discount + PS attach targeting adoption of 3 unadopted features ($320K base + $40K PS, ~25% margin), (b) 20% discount + 24-month lock with Year 2 step-up to $340K, or (c) usage-commit reset to $300K with tiered overage upside. CSM's current proposal to accept all three customer requests simultaneously is not approvable.

• *The 30% commit true-down sets a dangerous precedent.* Granting a full utilization-based reduction rewards underuse and hands every under-consuming mid-market account a negotiating playbook. Counter position: $320K (20% reduction) conditioned on a mandatory Success Accelerator for the 3 unadopted features within 60 days; or accept $280K floor only on a 24-month term with a contractual step-up. Current 12-month/$280K ask is commercially indefensible.

• *Account health (42/100) raises collectibility and precedent risk across all three clauses.* Net-60 on a distressed account elevates DSO and bad-debt exposure — counter with Net-45 or annual upfront invoicing. CFO must confirm the contract meets ASC 606 probable-collection threshold before revenue can be recognized. Northbeam's new Incrementality product launch is a positive signal, but the YC competitor at ~60% lower pricing and 3 of 5 features unadopted mean retention is not guaranteed even with the discount.

elements.text: AE: *Jamal Okafor* · Manager: *Marcus Reilly* · Close date: 2026-05-18 · review filed by Kiln · agent-driven

slack_post.plaintext_fallback:
FY26 Renewal — Northbeam Mortgage | ACV: $280K (30% off $400K list) | Est. margin: 14.3% — HARD BLOCK below 25% floor, CEO override required.
Deal must be restructured before submission: recommended paths are 20% discount + PS attach, 20% discount + 24-month lock, or usage-commit reset to $300K.
The 30% commit true-down sets a precedent rewarding underuse; counter at $320K with a mandatory adoption Success Accelerator condition.
Net-60 on a health-score-42 account elevates DSO and bad-debt risk; negotiate to Net-45 or annual upfront. CFO must validate ASC 606 collectibility.
Approval chain: AE Mgr → RevOps + Finance + CFO (parallel) → CEO · ~6 business days · AE: Jamal Okafor

ae_email_draft.subject: [Northbeam Mortgage] Deal review complete — BLOCKED, 3 things before you can submit
ae_email_draft.body_markdown:
Jamal, the deal-desk review is done and the Northbeam renewal is **hard-blocked** as structured: estimated gross margin at $280K is ~14.3%, well below our 25% absolute floor, and the deal cannot go to approval in its current form.

Here are the 3 things you must do before resubmitting:

1. **Reshape the discount — counter at $320K (20% off), not $280K (30% off).** The cleanest path is Option 3 from Pricing: 20% discount + a $40K PS attach targeting the 3 unadopted features, bringing TCV to $360K and margin back to ~25%. If the customer won't move, the fallback is accepting $280K only on a 24-month term with a $340K Year 2 step-up — do not sign a 12-month flat deal at $280K.
2. **Insert a renegotiation ratchet on the usage-based overage.** Pure usage pricing with no floor lets Northbeam ride upside consumption without ever stepping up to a larger commit. Counter language: mandatory commit renegotiation if overage exceeds 120% of Annual Commit in any rolling 90-day window, and subsequent renewal commit must be ≥ 90% of (prior commit + overage consumed).
3. **Push payment terms to Net-45 (not Net-60), or flip to annual upfront.** A health score of 42 on an account asking for extended terms is a bad combination — Finance and CFO will ask about this immediately. Annual upfront at $280K due within 60 days of execution is the cleanest counter and eliminates the DSO/collectibility risk that will otherwise delay rev-rec sign-off.

Once restructured, your approval chain is: **Marcus (AE Manager) → RevOps + Finance + CFO in parallel → CEO** — all five approvers, ~6 business days. CFO has veto; CEO has veto. Start with Marcus today so the parallel step can run while you're still negotiating with Northbeam's CRO. Close date is May 18 and you have no runway to let this sit.

customer_email_draft.subject: Northbeam FY26 Renewal — Our Proposal for a Path Forward
customer_email_draft.body_markdown:
We understand Northbeam's position: you came in under-utilized on the prior commit, a lower-priced option is on the table, and you need this resolved before your CRO signs off in two weeks. We want to find a structure that works for both sides, and we've done the internal work to get you a concrete counter quickly.

On the commit reduction: we can move to $320,000 ACV (a 20% reduction from $400K), but we're not in a position to go to $280K at a 12-month term — that structure doesn't pass our internal margin floor regardless of the retention value we place on Northbeam as a customer. The $320K figure is our floor for a standard 12-month renewal.

Conditioned on that number, we're prepared to pair the reduction with a dedicated Success Accelerator engagement to drive adoption of the three unadopted features within 60 days of signing — this directly addresses the utilization gap that drove the under-consumption, and it positions Northbeam to get meaningfully more value from the platform before the next renewal cycle.

On overage pricing: we'll accept the hybrid usage-based structure at $0.0008/unit above the commit, with one guardrail — if overage consumption exceeds 120% of the annual commit in any rolling 90-day window, that triggers a commit renegotiation at no worse than the current overage rate; we're not willing to leave expansion economics entirely open-ended given the utilization trajectory.

On payment terms: we can move to Net-45 for this renewal cycle; Net-60 is above what we can approve for this tier without additional sign-offs that would push past your deadline, and we'd prefer not to let payment terms become the blocker here.

We recognize the competitor conversation is real, but a YC-stage startup carries integration, support, and longevity risk that a $70M ARR fintech shouldn't underweight — we're happy to put that side-by-side if useful for your internal review.

To keep your CRO timeline intact, we'd like to get on a call with you by end of week to align on these terms and get a redlined order form to you no later than May 12. Can you confirm a 30-minute slot Thursday or Friday?

approval_review_one_pager.title: FY26 Renewal — Northbeam Mortgage Commit Reduction Request

sections.heading: Headline
sections.content_markdown: Northbeam Mortgage ($280K ACV, Series C fintech) is requesting a 30% commit reduction from $400K, a shift to usage-based overage, and net-60 payment terms at renewal. CSM proposes accepting all three to defend a flagship logo against a YC-backed competitor priced ~60% below current rates; the deal as structured is a **hard block** on gross margin and cannot proceed without a full reshape.

sections.heading: Pricing Summary
sections.content_markdown:
| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| List Price | $400,000 |
| Proposed Price | $280,000 |
| Effective Discount | **30%** (mid-market soft ceiling: 20%; hard ceiling: 30%) |
| Est. Gross Margin | **~14.3%** — violates the 25% absolute floor and 40% warn threshold |
| Payment Terms | Net-60 (requested up from Net-30) |

**Three alternative structures — all restore margin to ≥25%:**
- **Option A — 20% Discount + 24-Month Lock:** $320K, 24-mo term, 5% Year 2 escalator, one-time Net-45 grace → 25% margin, +$40K ACV vs. proposal.
- **Option B — Usage-Commit Reset w/ Tiered Overage:** $300K floor, 25% discount, hybrid tiered overage ($0.0008 → $0.0006 above 120%), Net-60 granted → 20% margin (still requires Finance approval).
- **Option C — 20% Discount + PS Attach (Recommended):** $320K base + $40K PS engagement targeting 3 unadopted features → $360K TCV, 25% blended margin, +$80K ACV vs. proposal; addresses root-cause adoption problem.

sections.heading: Risk Findings
sections.content_markdown:
**Pricing Guardrails — 2 hard failures:**
- 🔴 **BLOCK (Absolute):** Gross margin ~14.3% < 25% floor. Deal cannot execute as structured.
- 🟡 **WARN:** 30% discount breaches mid-market 20% soft ceiling; Finance sign-off mandatory at this boundary.

**Redline Flags:**
- 🔴 **High — Commit Reduction:** Accepting a 30% true-down to consumption sets a book-wide precedent rewarding under-utilization. Counter: $320K (20%) conditioned on 60-day Success Accelerator adoption sprint; fallback: $280K only on 24-month term with $340K Year 2 step-up.
- 🟠 **Medium — Usage-Based Overage:** Pure usage pricing with no renegotiation trigger forfeits expansion upside if Northbeam's new Incrementality product drives consumption recovery. Require mandatory renegotiation trigger at 120% of commit in any rolling 90-day window.
- 🟡 **Low — Net-60 Terms:** Elevated by 42/100 health score. Counter: Net-45 or annual upfront invoicing ($280K due within 60 days of execution) to eliminate mid-year cash float risk.

**ASC 606 Red Flags:**
- 🟡 **WARN:** 30% discount may set SSP precedent affecting future allocations.
- 🟡 **WARN:** Collectibility concern — health score 42, 3 of 5 features unadopted; CFO must confirm contract meets ASC 606-10-25-1(e) probable-collection threshold before recognizing revenue.
- ℹ️ Hybrid pricing shift requires billing/rev-rec system support for usage-based recognition. Net-60 financing component should be confirmed as immaterial.

sections.heading: Approval Routing
sections.content_markdown:
| Step | Approver | Veto? | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AE Manager (Marcus Reilly) | No | Standard entry |
| 2 (parallel) | RevOps | No | ACV $100K–$500K |
| 2 (parallel) | Finance | No | Non-standard payment terms |
| 2 (parallel) | CFO | **Yes** | Discount >25% |
| 3 | CEO | **Yes** | Gross margin absolute floor violation |

**Estimated cycle time: ~6 business days** — but the deal is **currently blocked pre-submission**. The gross margin violation must be resolved (deal restructured) before routing to CFO/CEO. Close date is May 18; 6-day cycle leaves ~1 week buffer if restructure conversations begin today (May 5).

sections.heading: Recommendation
sections.content_markdown:
**🔴 HOLD — Restructure Required Before Routing**

Do not approve the CSM proposal as submitted; counter with **Option C (20% discount + $40K PS attach)** to address root-cause adoption failure, recover $80K ACV versus the current ask, restore margin to the 25% floor, and give Northbeam's CRO a credible success narrative rather than a pure price concession — while the 24-month lock in Option A remains the preferred fallback if the customer resists the PS engagement.

comms.reasoning_summary: Calibrated customer email to warm tone given the deal context. AE email leads with verdict (ship-with-conditions (blockers must clear first)) and a numbered next-step list. One-pager and Slack post both headline the recommendation and surface 3 flagged redlines (3 countered in customer reply). Approval routing summary: ~6 business days through 5-step chain.

synthesis: Northbeam's $280K renewal hard-blocks on the gross margin floor — effective margin lands at ~14.3% against a 25% absolute floor, making the deal structurally non-viable as currently shaped. ASC 606 flags four issues, most notably collectibility risk tied to the 42 health score and SSP impact from the 30% discount, all of which require CFO/auditor validation. The redline picture is dominated by the 30% commit reduction: at this health score Clay absorbs a $120K ACV haircut with no structural recovery mechanism and risks setting a utilization-true-down precedent across the book. Approval routes AE Manager → RevOps/Finance/CFO → CEO over ~6 business days, but cannot move until the margin block is resolved — recommend hold and re-scope around the PS-attach alternative to address the underlying adoption problem before resubmitting.
